Chapter 20

About the Author

Fiction writing every day for 28 days.

Originally my first attempt at writing fiction was based on an offer from an established author.

Lucky me, right?

Here's a portion of her offer via email:

"How about this. You write a story every day for one month. A challenge. Time limited every day. Only write for one hour, or a half hour. That's it. Whatever it is, it is. Stream of consciousness is more than acceptable.

So what do you write about? Anything!

Thirty minutes up. Now I can have a nice cup of coffee and go on with my day.

I'm a writer who writes."

LINDA A. LAVID

For personal reasons, some of the 28 days of writing have been removed. Enjoy!

CHAPTER 1

Standing with my back against shelves of books I have never read and never will, I start to speak.

The tiny crowd coughs and adjusts while trying to get comfortable in those metal folding chairs.

Over eager eyes stare back, hanging on my every uttered syllable. As if I speak anywhere near as well as I write.

Writing takes time and only comes out of growth.

Speaking is in the now with no chance to edit. No do-overs.

I begin as usual with the giving of credit where credit is due. Always saying that my love Linda gifted me a wind up tomato shaped timer gadget which spawned a seemingly never ending series of books.

Each and every book a best seller, a light in the dark, life changing, songs were sung, wine and cheese served, yadda yadda yadda.

CHAPTER 2

There it sat on the carpeted floor, in total awe of the surroundings.

Only wearing a fully loaded cloth tightly fitted to its bottom, filling the room with that smell. It paused, pondering where to go next. A nearby cat struck a pose as if to say all of this was beneath them. Totally forgetting that once they had been a kitten.

Suddenly some unseen urge attracted it, a baby, to the only unprotected electrical outlet in the room. The race was on.

It began to crawl at break neck speed. Always amazing how fast we move when some unseen siren lures us into dangers. Having observed this pattern of behavior many times before, disaster was averted. Life often seems like a perpetual jar that is sometimes clear, sometimes cloudy, always and forever we look in or look out.

CHAPTER 3

Her email said "No! I do not like the word torpor!".

But I really do, it’s a cool word. Or at least I like the idea of going into a state of deep hibernation, a sleep without getting up to do anything, not even bodily functions like eating or pooping. Heck, hummingbirds do it, bees do it, and bears do it.

But all she wanted to talk about was why men no longer look at her the way they use to. It seems only a few years ago she complained about men looking at her like she was a side of beef. She insists on being way deeper than that, got it, she's deep. This despite most of her emails including a recently taken selfie. Always with a mirror in the background. Somehow. How many mirrors does she own? She always asks how she looks today, I say cute, but my heart is not in it.

Long ago we went steady, as they use to say, back in high school. In those days that meant hanging out together while working up the nerve to hold her hand. A bit like being a Puritan and a Victorian, all in one. Surely, even they had more fun.

She had literally tracked me down via my daughter, who directed her to my Facebook page. She "friended" me.

Is this how a stalk begins?

In time I "friended" her back, as I recognized her name and became certain it was the person I remembered. Then the emails came. An endless stream of thoughts ensued, her's not mine, as mine are way more than enough. Of course they were not deep at all but only her most mundane daily thoughts.

Time passed, years actually, with lots of emails exchanged. One day she slipped up and mentioned the hubby. Sure one can have married friends, but my question was is he aware and ok with our bordering on the intimate chitchats.

After a few quiet days, her response was that the hubby did not like this at all. So I "unfriended" her on Facebook. She went ballistic and sad, both at the same time. Her final email ended with the phrase "the dew has fallen from the lily" ... as it always does.

Our relationship entered torpor.

CHAPTER 4

Gina was a vampyre.

She stated this on our first date.

She stressed that she was not like the overly commercial Hollywood vampires, like in Buffy and movies.

Those were ridiculous.

She was the real deal, and wanted not just my blood but my soul.

Great, yet another Italian women in my life, I'm either blessed or cursed, not sure which. Plus I really only had myself, fun, and love to offer, and definitely not my soul.

Her was response was that's fine, we'll get to the other in time.

Our first date was at the airport. We worked at the same book store and she had asked me out. Despite her being much younger, yet always wearing long black Victorian dresses, I said yes. She was pretty with dark green eyes and long black hair and always serious, never a smile. By airport I mean we laid beside each other on the hood of my car at the end of a busy runway. We drank mead that she made herself while we watched airplanes takeoff. She loved evenings and that twilight time of day. We laid there making up stories about where the passengers were going. We talked for hours about traveling, likes and dislikes, pretty much first date stuff, except for the bits about vampyres. As first dates go, it was unique but nice. As evening became night then early morning, she suggested going to the Au Pied De Cochon for French onion soup. I had been to the foot of the pig many times before for late night or early morning soup. Those hot crocks of soup with cheese melted over the top were famous, as well as the myths about KGB spies defecting through their restroom windows. Afterwards we went to her apartment.

After our earlier talks, especially the vampyre bits, I expected her home to be dark with a touch of the sinister. It was that and more. There were lots of black candles, lots of melted wax, lots of stone gargoyles, lots of human skulls (they looked real), and red or black velour everywhere. It was a basement apartment with no windows so no way sunlight could ever enter, and no electric lights, just candle light. There was a huge canvas oil painting on a wall depicting some Maasai piercing, draining, and drinking fresh blood from a cow. As well as other gory artworks. There was that old musty basement smell too. On a book case mostly filled with very old untitled journals was two altered human skulls. They had been carved to fit a deck of cards in each skull, with a deck replacing the lower jaw in one, while another deck fit an opening above the eyes in the other. I think they were tarot decks but I'm not sure as she said I should never touch them. Of course the inevitable happens, blame on the mead, the pretty, or the unusual surroundings, but romance transcends all.

Time passed in pretty much same way as always until one evening she locked on to the topic of drinking my blood and reaping my soul. I was not keen on losing either one. She became a pit bull and just would not let it go. Of course, by this point in time I was kind of sort of in love with her. So I let her talk me through how, if I allowed it, this whole scenario would play out. The blood part sounded, well, bloody and painful, for me. The soul part, she said, was automatic after she drank a glass filled with my blood. To ease me into blood part of this, she offered to take a small amount from my right wrist. Not so bad, I thought, as I had given blood to the Red Cross and they take a pint. I said ok. At this she came close to smiling. And so the end began.

I had wondered about the necklace she always wore but never asked. Turns out it was a small dagger of a sort. A bit like an upside down ankh shape, but curved and made of bone or antler, with the loop being the handle. She pulled down and withdrew the blade. She sterilized it then poked it into my wrist. It was quick and painless really.

The things we do for love. Blood streamed out and into the palm of my hand. She pressed her face into my palm, lapping and slurping up my blood, moving slowly ever upward to the opening she had made in my wrist. Her lips wrapped around that tiny opening and she sucked hard and harder and harder. I pulled away. She cocked her head back and smiled as blood ran down her chin.

That was her first and only smile and an end to our relationship. I have never seen her since that evening. Gina was indeed a vampyre.

CHAPTER 5

A Blue Jay lay belly up on the ground.

Writhing and making gurgling sounds. Blood spurted from an eye creating a hallo of red around its head.

My first kill.

It was a lucky shot from over 30 yards away, and hit the bird in the head. A simple lead pellet fired from a pump action BB gun is all it took. Proudly I strutted over to see. For a brief moment I felt more adult than boy, manly even.

Squatting down over the bird and watching it slowly die, I felt a sudden flood of emotions and sick to my stomach. Any sense of luck or pride quickly vanished. I was filled with regret and a knowing that what just happened could never be undone, not ever. This bird was not killed for food, as we often did with chickens and pigs on the farm. Life on a farm often seemed full of meanness on purpose, which I never liked being a part of. My first kill had a nonchalant meanness about it, boyish. I cried. Promising myself to never repeat such a thing. My first kill haunts me to this day, reliving it anytime I see a bird sitting on a limb off in the distance.

CHAPTER 6

Plum sat at her desk for exactly eight hours every work day for almost forty years.

An hour commute in to her work cubicle and an hour to get back home. Plum was a nickname, origin unknown, her real name was Edna Plummley.

She transcribed audio recordings of meetings onto paper using an electric typewriter. It was fast and so was she. Her grammar and spelling was the envy of coworkers. Whereas they relied on a book by Webster, kept beside their typewriters, she did not. Her Webster's remained in a desk drawer for many years now. A source of pride. She was an avid reader and collector of words. She was about to retire and wondered how to fill those empty ten hours per day soon to come. She hoped her nightly obsession would not take over her whole day.

Plum could not remember how or when this obsession had started. Perhaps after her first and only husband ran off with another man. So many years of being alone had turned her into a spinster. Could all of that loneliness have conjured this fixation of hers?

Initially she tried to write about that recurring scene in her diary:

Doc said I should avoid all avid Diva's. The cod they serve is acid to the soul.

Do not fall into their void. Theirs is a false light offering no aid during strange times.

Just say "Ciao!" and walk away.

But not only did such writing not capture the scene from her mind, the words just did not make any sense. She turned to art.

Trying to depict that scene using pencil/pen/ink line drawings, watercolors, oils, and most recently acrylic paintings. She even returned to the site to take photographs to use as reference for her art. Her home became filled with the exact same image from every angle and in every style imaginable. It had definitely been captured with hundreds of renderings scattered all about her home. She could stop herself from repeating the process over and over again.

The scene was from Plum's childhood. As a young girl she had lived with an uncle for a few years. Why, she did not remember. There was something spellbinding about that scene, even though it was only made up of simple things: a shed, a rock, and the path leading there. She clearly recalls sitting on that smooth flat rock near the entrance to the shed. For hours and hours, as if time had stopped. In time she began to wonder if there was more to this obsession.

Something had been forgotten or buried deep down inside her. It lacked context, like a phantom on the periphery of understanding.

An unseen attractor beckoned her onward.

CHAPTER 7

Others often remarked that he lead a charmed life.

They said he was blessed to be so talented. Blessed not to have to grind out a living at some mundane job. He did not agree. His so called talent was a direct result of application, practice, and will power. Nothing more. He could not remember a time when he did not play a guitar of some sort. His first guitar was one his mother had learned on, but she had given up playing to raise a family. The guitar and lessons were a special gift from her. She told him he had an ear for music.

This lead to music school and a long list of bands, touring, and writing his own private songs. A life filled with booze, women, and song. Often all at the same time. Women were his muse, so many of them he said were just "a bit of all right". A refrain he repeated in many of his songs. He knew that those words were probably by Bob Dylan, as so many lyrics are. No relationship ever lasted more than a few months at best. He had an uncontrollable temper. It was even worse after a long night of drinking and performing. Muses paid for getting too close. Jail time seemed to deepen his music and appeal to women.

Over the years, a few times, he caught a glimpse of a repeated pattern in his life. Only to smother it in more song, booze, and women.

Unable to cope with looking directly at it. Years passed. Age happened. Nowadays he covered the songs of other artists during performances, at weddings and bars. Women had all but disappeared, especially the younger ones. There was a growing sense of realizing that a rut had built up around his life. He wondered if this was regret or just a feeling of being trapped in the blue hour. His latest song "The Dark Night of the Soul" seemed to be writing itself, but probably already done by Dylan as well.

CHAPTER 8

Mia lived on the edge of a very small town.

A short walk away was a grocery store and the public library. She never learned to drive so did not own a car, and had no desire to travel. She had grown up and still lived in a house she inherited from her aunt. Whom she had called mother. Mother passed away just after she graduated from high school. There was no other family that she knew of. And she never knew how or why her aunt had raised her. Mia was the only person at her aunt's funeral. Her only semblance of a social life was limited to very brief interactions at the store or the library. She had never been on a date, no lovers, no children, no boyfriends or girlfriends.

Mother had hoarded candles and books of crossword puzzles. Thousands of them, maybe more. They were everywhere. Mother could never bear to solve those puzzles, entrapping words in those tiny boxes felt wrong to her. So all of the puzzles remained empty, left bare for Mia to fill in. After electricity was installed in the house, mother could not bear to burn the candles anymore either. Mia spent much of her time filling in the crossword puzzles. In the evening she preferred the warm glow of candlelight while mulling over a puzzle.

Mother's pride and joy had been a hard bound set of Oxford dictionaries that included a magnifying glass to read the tiny print on the thin rice paper pages. Mother never touched them and forbade Mia as well. Now, Mia used them every day, to collect words in journals, which helped with the puzzles. The only books she owned was Catcher in the Rye, A Clockwork Orange, and Practical Magic. It was probably the cover art on these books that had caught her eye, but she was never sure why she bought only those three. She didn't even like them, preferring the used library books.

Sometimes on her short daily walks to the store and library, she would catch herself reflecting on her life versus the characters she read about in books. Do people really do all of those things? Is she odd, weird, or eccentric? Always returning to her daydreaming about today's crossword puzzle and what was that long word at 15 across. Once she even pondered about her name backwards, Mia is aim. She liked that, aim, to zero in on just the correct word to scribble it's letters into those tiny boxes. Her life felt full. Habitual and repetitive, sure. Occasionally she did wonder if living vicariously through words and stories was enough.

CHAPTER 9

There I was neck deep in pond water.

I seemed myself but different somehow. More instinctual. I burped, a lot, or something like burping. My life had changed the moment I met her. She had called me to mend a fence and install a new faucet. I did handyman work for a living. Not much of a living, but enough for rent, beer, chips, and cable TV. What else is there? Oh yeah, sex, of course. Enter Kass. Kass, aka Kassanova, was a tall lean yet shapely young woman with flaming red hair and bright green eyes. She was drop dead gorgeous. And she was looking for a lot more handy work than just a fence and faucet. This she made very clear from the get go. So after fixing the fence and faucet, I gave it my best at fixing her too. A dream come true except she was insatiable. And a bit into everything. Things I didn't know existed. Things I did not like. She would coax me on by saying my name all sexy like. “Again, Paris of Troy, again!” Yes, my first and middle names were Paris and Troy. Mom was a big fan of Greek myths, especially the one with Helen of Sparta and Paris of Troy and Aphrodite. You know, that crowd. Thanks mom. After several rounds between the bed posts, Kass declared that I belonged to her. Forever. I disagreed. Biggest mistake of my life.

I had noticed between bouts of sex, that her home contained some unusual things. Lots of sparkly colorful crystals, even more candles, and shelves of old leather bound books. Her kitchen was filled with vials and bottles of this and that. She called them potions and tinctures, I think. But I failed to put two and two together. Suddenly one evening, she announced that she was a witch, and that I would do what she wanted or else. Now she looked much older, a lot less attractive. Kind of serious. So I was ready to leave. Yet I froze in my tracks, unable to move. Her voice got a lot deeper uttering strange words and growl like noises. I didn't understand. The room got darker and candles burst to light forming a circle around me. She danced and pranced around the edge of the circle growling and speaking gibberish. I was trapped. After a while she entered the circle and kissed me on the lips. When she backed away, she seemed much taller. A giant. Everything got blurry.

So that was how I came to be in this pond. Very intent on catching a bug with only my tongue. I could feel Kass coming to check on me, her steps sounded like thunder making ripples in the water I sat in. She bent over and in a booming voice said "How's Paris, my little froggy, today?"

CHAPTER 10

I often said to Liz, "Hey, sweetness and light, how's tricks?".

She glowed a tiny blush. Every time. Something about those freckles just below her eyes combined with her blond hair and dark eyes hit a spot for me. Her voice too. This was desire, lust, and love, all packaged in a twenty something woman. But I was almost twenty years older. So my feelings felt odd, misplaced, disconnected. Not to mention that she was very well read, an intellectual. A master's degree in English Literature, she said. Her a talker. I was physical, movement, not all up in my head. I had a BS degree in Computer Science and not a dreamer. We were opposites in many ways.

We became acquainted while working part-time at a bookstore. I managed the store. She purchased the books. So we interacted often. A relationship, of sorts, grew. I became concerned about her walking alone to her apartment after work. The store closed at 1AM everyday, which seemed a dangerous thing for her to do alone. So I walked her home most nights. There was a comfort in her voice on those long, often intentionally roundabout, walks. I rarely understood much of what she was on about, politics or history or whatever, but I enjoyed her voice and being with her. Occasionally we did kiss. Those kind of kisses where the world and time itself seems to fall away. For a brainiac, she was a great kisser. She never once invited me inside. This state of affairs laster for many months.

One evening we went for dinner at a nice outdoor restaurant. We agreed that there would be no talk of the bookstore, no gossip, and no alcohol. I sensed what she was about to say but did not cut her off at the pass. Let her say it, her way, in that moment. She did, slowly, gently. For once, not so intellectual, but emotional. She struggled with the words, a rarity for her. Essentially she did not want to fall in love, with me or anyone. She thought she felt love creeping up. She was not ready for 'till death do us part and all that jazz. I let her talk it out. Only listening, nodding, and probably betraying my inner feelings somehow.

After that dinner we worked together for two more years. I continued to walk her home, sometimes. She talked. I listened, the same as many nights before. We did not hold hands nor kiss, ever again. Neither did we speak of our other relationships. Some of whatever there was before no longer remained. It had fallen away.

CHAPTER 11

As he rocked in his chair on the porch, Spurgeon found his thoughts drifting back to his childhood.

A time best forgotten. He thought all that strangeness had been forgotten long ago. But all of it had returned, like ocean tides, in and out of memory. Now a daily assault of what's, why's, and for's appeared in his afternoon catnaps.

The strangeness of youth mostly centered around his mother. His father was just a mean person that was mostly absent. As he was a traveling salesman prone to temper and violence when he was at home, which was rare. His father was unpleasant but easy to understand, keep quiet and stay out of his way. On the other hand, mother was always home. Looking like she just got out of bed. Always stumbling about wrapped in layers of silk. He pictured her now like one of those tipsy women in those 1940’s black and white movies. Always dolled up for romance with a glass of gin and tonic in hand. She was all that and far worst.

His mother was drunk most of the time, which wasn't so bad as she was at least a happy touchy drunk. But a very manipulative one. He remembered the photos taped on the outside and inside of the refrigerator, reminding him to eat all his food. Those pictures were enough to put you off food forever. Often placed just in front of his plate of food, there was a framed photo of a starving child in a far away land. Always the child was about his age it seemed. As if to say "That kid would eat all his food, why won't you?". When upset about whatever, mostly suspicious of dad's whereabouts, she would lock Spurgeon in the basement. The basement was dark and empty with puddles of water that had seeped from the walls. It smelled bad. There was nothing down there that could do any real harm, just a boy's imaginings, yet it made him cry every time. He recalled huddling near the locked door and poking fingers under it and out into the light. Hoping. Sometimes she would step on his fingers with her bedroom slippers. Painful, yet it was oddly comforting all the same. The food and various punishments continued for many years.

During his high school years, mother's manipulation changed to the bizarre. Often she would sit on the floor, so he did as well, as she observed and communicated with the frolicking tiny pink elephants she saw encircling us. Not once did he see anything. He thought she was faking all this strangeness, for pity's sake, to keep him forever bound in her world. This escalated into contrived late night hysteria and panic attacks by her. After dark sometimes there would be loud knocks at the front door. Running to see, there would be mother crying with a handwritten note in her hands. She said no one was there just the note she held. She looked scared. The note said that she must divorce father immediately. Or someone named Olly was going to shoot and kill both mother and him. She showed me the note. The handwriting looked like mother's. Each time this happened mother somehow managed to whip the event up into a frenzy of panicked activity. He remembered the feeling of being afraid and being swept along with mother. This always ended with driving around in the car and eventually stopping at a fast food restaurant. Having milkshakes and french fries. Just to calm down then returning home.

Spurgeon did leave home after high school and never saw his father or mother ever again. Childhood had been survived and that strangeness had passed. Until one day he had received a birthday card in the mail. The envelope did not have a return address. Inside there was a card with a picture of a pink elephant floating in the air holding a blue balloon.

It read "Happy Birthday son, Love Dad & Olly". That handwriting looked familiar.

Back came a flood of memories best never revisited.

CHAPTER 12

Havre Dulac Grace is the name on his credentials.

He never liked his name, what was his parents thinking. He worked as a track and capture agent for the Taropian Enforcement Agency. Everyone just called it TEA. All of the agents had been law enforcement detectives, back when law and order was necessary. Nowadays all things were connected, literally. Once the teat, a binding life force entity thing, was manifested by the Mysterians, humanity and planet Earth changed forever. He was never sure he understood what teat was or how it worked, but it did. Precognition was as easy as breathing. You even knew what your cat was thinking.

Before the teat was released the Mysterians had been diviners and tarot readers. But somehow morphed over time, attaining true enlightenment, and discovering the teat. Once the teat got out, there was no putting the genie back in the bottle. Not easily. It bound everything and all life forms into a single collective of one easy access mind. Everything was plug and play now, with no privacy, no lying, and nothing on the down low. This change put law enforcement, justice, government, and the military out of business. The internet as well as broadcast radio and TV disappeared. All of those things were no longer needed. A sort of utopia was created and everything happened in your mind because of the teat force.

But as with all groups of people over the ages a split had formed. Factions broke off from the Mysterians. There now existed the Inners, Traders, Colabs, and Casters. None of them were as powerful as the Mysterians. These new groups pretended to be unaffected by the teat. But the teat connected all, whether you believe it or not. They preferred the old ways of divining by using tarot cards, omens seen, voices heard, or the casting of lots. Their numbers were small as their ways were seen as useless, now as ridiculous as belief and faith. In time another faction formed calling itself the Dires.

As former Mysterians the Dires had thorough knowledge of how the teat force worked. And over time, as a group working in unison, had developed a method to manipulate the teat within certain individuals. Once the Dire targeted a person their future was altered ending in their death. They simply put false information into the target's mind. Dires could perform this in a covert way, mostly covering their actions from the collective. Evil, of a sort, had returned. This is where Havre and others like him at TEA came in.

The Mysterians had secretly created a process to remove the teat from a person. Along with a drug, a person could extend the time spent outside the influence of the teat. They became as people once were, and no longer aware of being connected to everything. Havre did not like the process, as it took days in quarantine. Totally alone, in the dark, no voices in your head, and that old feeling of not knowing. Uncertainty returned, as you were slowly disconnected and unplugged. But the process was required otherwise the Dires would simply target a TEA agent. Before the process had been perfected, many agents lost their lives by being in the wrong place at the wrong time. Dire consequences.

On the teat, then off the teat, became the pattern of life for Havre. It was confusing and exhausting. Once off the teat, he and other agents reverted to using the skills they had mastered as former detectives. Handcuffs, interrogations, and all. Along with a device provided by the Mysterians that could detect false thoughts within the collective.

There was a limit to the number of times a person could be taken off the teat.

This was Havre's last time before the force that was the teat would resist the process. Winking in and out of different realities as he had done for many years, Havre was unsure about his future. A strange feeling in a world where your future was a certainty.

CHAPTER 13

She had walked right on past twilight.

It was too dark to see much of anything. No moon, no stars, just rain. Tired and hungry she found a tree just off the path to sit against. A taste of freedom occurred to her. For the first time in her life there wasn't anything she really had to do. The rain poured, the tears poured, her thoughts poured. With her back against an old tree she let it all flow through and around. There were thoughts, worries really, about an unknown future. She let those flow away as well. She fell asleep.

After waking, she decided that from now on her name would be Belles. She liked the sound of it. The rain had stopped, the path was muddy, the mud would slow her down but it felt soft on her feet. So off to town she went. What to do next, she decided, was left in the hands of fate. For now, she would notice all of the things around her as she walked. The sun popping in and out between the clouds, the mud squishing between her toes, the songs of birds glad the rain had ceased, and the splashing sounds of water rushing over rocks in a nearby stream. Appreciating the surroundings seemed to alleviate her suffering. Being chilled, damp, hungry, and naked was all still there, but her steps seemed lighter and quicker today. She wondered if this might be a thing called hope.

As she reached the outskirts of town, it seemed best to not stay out in the open. A naked woman draws too much attention, especially from men, even the so called honorable ones. She vaguely recalled delivering bacon to a widow and her daughter on the edge of town. One of her rare trips into town with Zeke. Discretely she traipsed through a couple of backyard gardens. Stopping and squatting down in a garden near what looked like a row of carrots. She thought this might be the place.

She was about to pull up a carrot when an old woman's voice commanded her to stop.

She froze.

CHAPTER 14

There she laid. Sleeping again.

She slept a lot. On the floor, or curled in a chair, or a sofa either upside down on her back or on her side. She never watched TV, as if it wasn't even turned on and didn't exist. Just staring at us while we locked eyes onto that TV tube. Let your eyes linger too long into her eyes and she was triggered. As if your eyes had said come, let's play. Once begun, it always played out the same. Slobbery exchanges of various toys; balls, stuffed squeaky animals, and rope pulling and tugging.

Her name was Molly. A mix of Lab and Husky. One blue eye, one milky brown eye. Slightly overweight at 90 pounds, otherwise perfectly happy in demeanor. I remember her tail thumping and pounding the floor as she lay half asleep upon my approach. Every time, for over fifteen years. She was a nice old big dog who loved everyone she met. If you fell asleep on the sofa, you would awake to her somehow being sprawled on top of you. Love on all fours.

For the most part, she was the same as every other pet dog I've known, with one exception. Molly came up with this on her own, no training. It happened every time, just let her out into the backyard, a sort of self walkies plus me being lazy sometimes, then I would sit on the porch steps watching. After doing her business, she would always dash back up the steps then turn around. Approaching from behind, she would force her head between my body and arm.

Imposing a hug.

We would just sit there in a hug and watch the world go by. I miss her.

CHAPTER 15

Lorelei was adorable.

Cute, petite, nice, sweet, with a light bulb shaped head, very short spiked hair except for a long braided rat tail down to her bottom. Also nice. Every week a different, all natural, dyed hair color. She always whispered when she spoke. But she seemed totally unaware of all of that, which made her all the more adorable. Well, to me. She was an avid reader of everything, it seemed. Apparently lots of avid readers are also drop outs from college. That's how we met. I was a night manager at a bookstore. She shelved books and helped customers find books to read. Most of the employees were drop outs or just adrift in one of life's doldrums.

In time we hung out more and longer. I may have been falling for her. But some things bugged me. First, every day she felt compelled to reassess where our relationship stood. She liked me yesterday, but not so much today. She liked me more today than yesterday. She thought she might be falling in love, yet unsure it was love, exactly. And so on. She expected me to respond in kind, but I never knew what to say. I liked her, she was adorable, comfy, and cozy. Time will tell, I thought. Second, she was absolutely certain that she could only really truly fall in love with someone who took a stand in life. An activist, a revolutionary, a radical. I thought most of those folk die young. Then she expressed a strong attraction to young Neo-Nazi men. She liked that they were tall, skinny, tattooed, and stern looking. I felt her slipping away.

Soon we became just friends again. Sometimes we took work breaks together. I snacked and listened as she whispered about how Neo's were very different than older Nazi's. She insisted that times have changed. She was still adorable, mostly.

CHAPTER 16

Covered in the salt of sea and sweat, baked by the sun, I was crisped all over.

Somehow I was adrift upon a sea in a dinghy with few supplies. I had memories, but none about the how's and why's of being here. It seemed there was a natural current, a flow to the water, but I was bound and determined to paddle in the opposite direction. A challenge with only one oar and no sail. I felt confused and lost but not anxious or worried. By day I seemed to be moving forward, a tiring effort. By night, with sleep, I seemed to drift back again. Stubbornly I wanted to escape this doldrums no matter the cost. Every day, the same again.

One evening my daily ever repeating pattern was broken. As I rested, drifting backwards yet again, I saw a large sailboat approaching. It glistened in the moonlight, all spit shined and polished. Rescued, at last, despite it heading in the opposite direction of my stubbornness. As it drew nearer I could see the vessel bristled with canons and flew the Jolly Roger flag. Not an encouraging sign, but what threat was a lone exhausted man in a one oared dinghy. None. Drifting along her starboard side, I could see people onboard, most were clutching the side railing. Strangely, without thinking about it, I recognized them all as famous writers of fiction. How could I know this, for I wasn't well read, as best as I could recall.

As I slipped past and upon a closer view it was as if I knew each one of them personally, from somewhere or sometime, I knew not. At the bow was Poe holding up a lantern staring at some unseen thing out beyond the horizon. A picture of melancholy was he. Just aft of Poe was Bronte, she gazed at him like an unrequited love. Her heart felt broken. Next came Shelley looking greenish bent over the railing. Her hair was being held back by Stoker as she wretched. Further down in the hull, below deck, I noticed flickering lights and movements in several portholes, one framed the scared face of the King of horror himself. Then my eyes caught clamberings amongst the masts, ropes, and sails. Our champion Vonnegut was maddeningly lashing this and battening that. Now I drifted passing two lovers, perhaps, on the stairs to the helm. It was Hemingway with an arm around Lavid. She was bedecked in pinks of all shades and sorts. Silks, cottons, and velvets all a fluttering in the wind. He jibbed in her ear Dolly, more telltales of flash fiction! Upon reaching the helm I saw that the skipper at the big wheel was Melville, seemingly continuing his age old arguments with a phantom Starbucks. At the back of the stern holding a big brass telescope was a silent Yeats. He seemed to be peering at the moon.

Tonight's moon was a super sized one, casting pinkish and orangish hues in the heavens and the sea. As it sailed away from me I saw painted on her stern "H.M.S. Purrlitzer", an unlikely name for either a pirate or a ghost ship. I had not cried out to be rescued. I laid back gazing at the moon and the stars with a smile. The doldrums are just fine, I resumed my stubbornness.

CHAPTER 17

There we laid on our sides, back to front or front to back, depending.

Like spoons she called it. Her favorite position she said. Though I was never quite certain who it was that I was spooning with. She was an actress, always in this or that character. A living rehearsal she called it. I found it confusing. I wanted to like her, to get to know her, not all of them, just her.

Her name was Cherri, pronounced like sherry, she said. We met at a little theater community play she performed in, one of Shakespeare's I think. At the after party I told her she was great. She seemed to soak up praise, genuine or not, like a sponge. I had noticed her before as an assistant in a college theater class. She was a senior and I was a sophomore. As a townie, she lived nearby off campus in her own house. All alone, except the house was filled with books and clothes. Reading, dressing up, and pretending was her life. Being with her was a nice change from dormitory life. I felt a bit spoiled. Perhaps I was really only attracted to her eccentricities, she was unlike anyone I have ever fallen for. Maybe I was mesmerized or spellbound, enchanted in some way I did not understand. In time that feeling would fade. As I could never get her to be her true self, or if she ever was herself, I could not tell.

She reminded me of the story of Sybil. While fiction, it was a story about a functioning person with multiple personalities. Similar to a disorder I had read about in a psychology course. Near the end of my relationship with Cherri, we talked about mental disorders. She had very firm opinions about disorders. Questioning what was disorder and what was order, both subjective she said. Stating that some people are aware that certain choices in life may lead to an appearance of disorder. Many years later I would come to understand and appreciate some of what she had said.

She remains a fond memory.

I liked her, but never really knew her, or any of them, not really.

CHAPTER 18

Mornings before school were a balancing act.

Unmatched by even the Cirque Du Soleil. Most days there was a tiny horde of ten to thirteen kids, along with a cat and dog, all bellied up to the trough awaiting to be fed. Five of those kids were the fruit of my loins. The others were their freeloading friends. Each of them had their own breakfast vices. But mostly they all wanted an omelette, in all the ways those could be created. Moments such as this are possibly best remembered in the future, when the details get tinted by that rose colored fog that only occurs in memories.

A daydream had taken me back to those times. Bringing back feelings of being hectic and scatterbrained. The dream waxed and waned with a swirl of remembered activity, once commonplace, now seemingly an impossible task at the break of dawn. In and out came the rumblings of youth, the whisking of eggs in separate bowls mixed with water or milks, closely watching those four fry pans on the stovetop while butter or oil sizzled, then the finale of wielding a spatula for the plating. All to appease the hungry. I watched while they wolfed it down as if this was not a daily thing. The night always reset them. That flurry always ended with some chow for the awaiting cat and dog, who had already begged morsels of omelette from a few of the sympathetic. Begging works, if you have fur and a tail. I never ate. There wasn't time. Now it was off to school for them all. Then back home to clean up, make lists, ensure tomorrow was a repeat performance.

Even with the blessing of daydreaming, it all seemed distant and devoid of long ago emotions. Something was missing. Once awake again I did recall an old thought. Those days always seemed as if from a fairytale with kids starring as dwarves. Each dwarf playing out in various ways some aspect of my own persona and moods. Still something was missing.

CHAPTER 19

Some memories seem as if from a past life, time changes so many things.

There is a memory that lingers, vividly, an iridescent shimmer of a series of firsts. Her name was Sue. We were both seniors in high school. Different high schools, she attended a Catholic private school, me a public school. We actually met while doing volunteer work at a hospital. She was a candy striper delivering flowers to patients. I assisted burn victims receiving fitness and wax therapies. Sue was a tall tanned long legged blonde, friendly, but a bit of a tomboy. She was the first girl I ever worked up the nerve to ask out on a date. A real date, no chaperone, in a car borrowed from my uncle.

Our first date, she decided, would be a picnic. I had no clue what that meant. But there we were barefoot on a blanket in a park with a basket of goodies her mother put together. We talked and nibbled on nuts dipped in chocolate, cheeses, crackers, and sliced fruits. To me she seemed smart, smarter than me, but she was a good listener, and not chatting away endlessly. Often we were silent, just looking into each other's eyes. Our feet touched sometimes, tingles. Soon her feet were doing all sorts of things. She had talented toes, I think this was my first footsies. There were a lot of firsts with Sue. Kissing her was definitely a first, it was like she fell or moved away during a kiss. A swoon in motion. I asked her out again. This time I had a plan. She said yes.

My school had discount tickets to the autumn carnival and fair. Neither of us had ever been before. Yet another first. We did all the usual stuff: Ferris wheel, carousel, roller coaster, cotton candy, funnel cake, and so on. Our favorite was a very fast circular undulating ride that squashed her into me, continuously playing the song "Magic Carpet Ride" by Steppenwolf. We liked watching the crusty carny guy hopping on and off as the ride spun around at 60 mph. Yes, there was some high speed centrifugal force kissing, except this time she fell into me, not away. Another first. Bored of the rides and sugars we explored the sideshow row of tents. Nothing really appealed, it all seemed very snake oil salesman, so fake. She noticed some elephants chained to stakes in an alleyway behind the sideshow row. As we sympathized with elephants, she noticed a smaller tent with a smoky indigo glow about it. A handwritten sign on the tent's flap said PSYCHIC, enter please. We looked at each other, held hands, and in we went.

Once inside there was a large round table covered by a dark indigo cloth with needlepoint stars and a full moon. A crystal ball was in the center with a stack of cards laying atop a white silk scarf. The only light was from thickly dripping candles placed all about. The smell in the air was new to me, it wasn't perfume but what I now know as incense. Since my grandmother was a tarot reader I did kind of recognize the size of the cards, but the backs were new to me. Sue did not know about my grandmother and her tarot readings, nor my interest in tarot. From behind a beaded curtain entered an older lady covered in colorful veils and jewelry and tattoos. As if stepping from a gypsy wagon. She said to please call her Madame, and she only accepted donations not payment. Madame asked if one of us had a question for her. Madame also never stopped staring at me. Did I know her, did my grandmother know her, I wondered. Sue looked at me, I suppose because I was paying for everything, I shook my head no. Sue thought. While Madame riffle shuffled, Sue asked a question.

Would we marry and live happily ever after?

Madame stopped shuffling and placed the deck on the table face down. She asked Sue to cut the cards into three piles. Sue did, as she glanced at me sideways, I suppose, to gauge my reaction to her question. I had none, a bit startled perhaps. Madame stared at me, as if angry now, as she gathered up the piles into one again. I do remember feeling over heated, maybe from the rides and too much sugar. Madame now fanned out all of the cards in a perfect arc on the table. Her hands went back and forth over the cards as she plucked one from here and another from there. She placed seven cards face down in a horseshoe shape with the opening facing me and Sue. My grandmother had never done it this way, that I knew of. Yet another first.

Madame's interpretation of those cards led her to say that no we would not marry and further that our lives would not be all that happy, whether we stayed together or not. She was blunt, succinct, and to the point. No snake oil or sugar coating in her sideshow. I donated ten dollars. Sue seemed sad and left the tent. I lingered to ask Madame what that tarot deck was, it was the Thoth by Aleister Crowley. She said thank you for the donation, then added how disappointed it made her that I had not asked the question that was in my heart. I simply left to find Sue. There she stood, changed somehow. We left. I took her home. I never saw her again. Brief moments from long ago, a simple memory, nothing profound, nothing spectacular, yet it's shimmer lingers.

CHAPTER 20

There I was doing something way out of character for me.

A live chat stream interview on YouTube with two guests. Never saw that coming. My guests were a man, I suspected of being a wizard, and his pet Cockatiel bird. The man's name was Wobeegon and he was at least seven feet tall. Always with a long cane, he called a wand, that had a large clear crystal on top. Covering his left arm was a sleeve of multiple layers of thick leathers to protect his skin from the bird's claws and beak. She, the bird, was named Smidgens. She talked, a lot. Sometimes carrying on what felt like real conversations.

So there we were; interacting in a live chat, talking about this and that, reading back viewers comments, but mostly listening to Smidgens talk. It took a handful of tough to crack nuts to keep her quiet. The wizard wanted to try something on camera. This live chat already seemed strange enough, now what, but first some background.

Wobeegon and Smidgens spent much of their daylight hours walking around our tiny town. That's how we met, since I jog everyday our paths crossed many times over the years. Our first meeting was on a very hot summer day. I had jogged around the high school track that day with my shirt off. Because of childhood scars on the left side of my back, I don't go shirtless very often in public. It draws too much attention, questions, all of which lead to bad memories. There I was doing some after run stretches at the parallel bars when a big white bird landed on a bar. It eyed me then blurted out all at once

"Hello. Wazzup? What's your name? Me Smidgens".

I stood up and looked around. As if out of nowhere, right behind me, there was a very tall man with a odd looking cane. Smidgens said "Why rude?". I was startled but introduce myself to both bird and man. He noticed my back, but only remarked about the faint fern-like pattern scars. He recognized them as scars from lightning or high voltage shocks. I told him that I had experience both of those many years ago. He had some thoughts about people and the affects of after shocks, as he called them. His opinion was that once shocked you were more susceptible to future shocks. This would all lead to his experiment during the YouTube live chat, so back to the interview.

Smidgens remained mostly quiet while fretting over those nuts. Wobeegon proposed to conjure some static electricity using his wand, the cane. To be forever captured on video, maybe. I said sure, fully aware that if it did work it would only be a weak piezoelectric effect and display, so not all that dangerous. Except for a candle, I turned off the lights. We both stood up. I refocused the camera to capture everything. We were about three feet apart. He grab the cane by the crystal ball on top, touched me in the chest with the other end. Pulled it back then sharply stabbed it into the floor. Then a faint bluish white bolt emitted from the crystal cracking dead bang into my chest near my heart. It was more intense than I expected, dazed me, but surprised me mostly. I felt a warm spot near my heart, so I pulled down the neck of my tee shirt to reveal an oval reddish rash-like spot about palm size. This is when Smidgens said, as clear as could be, "Christopher, never trust a wizard!". The wizard had sat down, now with a sinister look on his face.

Alas, the video went viral, as they say, with millions of views.

I vanished from YouTube, that was my last video.

ABOUT THE AUTHOR

This book is my first attempt at writing very short stories, vignettes. Most of them are based on true life events but embellished to be more readable as a story. You will of course notice many mistakes: grammar, sentences fragments, and an overall lack of craft and style. However, more experienced and established authors have read these stories and generously offered their encouragement. Hopefully a day will come when craft and style begin to take form. So, at this point, I wanted to document my current state of affairs as a writer, and someday it might be entertaining to look back on this first effort. How many authors have been brave or stupid enough to have done this?

For more information:

www.slipthetrap.com

Digital collage artworks:

slip-the-trap.pixels.com

For all your freely given help, with love to, Linda A. Lavid.
